task: I need help putting together a quick checklist for comparing a repair estimate against the work order before I approve anything. Please keep it practical and easy to scan, since I’ll probably be using it while I’m looking at the paperwork.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a quick, practical checklist you can use side-by-side with the estimate and work order.

## Repair Estimate vs. Work Order Checklist

### 1) Basic Info
- [ ] Your name / vehicle or item info is correct
- [ ] Estimate and work order numbers match or are linked
- [ ] Date, shop name, and contact info are correct
- [ ] Mileage / serial number / asset details are accurate

### 2) Problem Description
- [ ] The issue described on the work order matches what you reported
- [ ] Any notes about symptoms, damage, or inspection findings make sense
- [ ] No extra problems are listed without explanation

### 3) Parts
- [ ] All parts on the estimate appear on the work order
- [ ] Part numbers match, if listed
- [ ] Brand/quality level matches what was quoted
- [ ] No added parts you didn’t approve
- [ ] Core charges, shipping, or handling fees are shown clearly

### 4) Labor
- [ ] Labor tasks on the estimate match the work order
- [ ] Labor hours are the same or clearly explained if changed
- [ ] Hourly labor rate matches the quote
- [ ] Diagnostic time is included and explained

### 5) Pricing
- [ ] Total estimate matches the work order
- [ ] Taxes are correct
- [ ] Fees are itemized and understandable
- [ ] Discounts, coupons, or warranties are applied correctly
- [ ] Any price increase is explained before approval

### 6) Scope of Work
- [ ] The work order includes only the repairs you agreed to
- [ ] No “recommended” or optional work is included as required
- [ ] Any add-on services are clearly marked optional
- [ ] Subcontracted work is disclosed if applicable

### 7) Authorization
- [ ] Approval is only for the work you want done
- [ ] Any “not-to-exceed” limit is written down, if relevant
- [ ] You understand what happens if additional issues are found
- [ ] You know whether they must call for approval before extra work

### 8) Warranty / Guarantees
- [ ] Warranty terms are listed
- [ ] Warranty covers parts, labor, or both
- [ ] Warranty length is clear
- [ ] Any exclusions are noted

### 9) Timing
- [ ] Estimated start and completion dates are listed
- [ ] Delays or backordered parts are noted
- [ ] Pickup or return instructions are clear

### 10) Red Flags
- [ ] Vague wording like “as needed” or “miscellaneous” is explained
- [ ] Duplicate charges are not present
- [ ] Same item isn’t billed twice under different names
- [ ] “Shop supplies” or similar fees are reasonable and disclosed
- [ ] Anything you don’t understand is clarified before signing

## Quick rule of thumb
If it’s **not on the estimate, not on the work order, or not clearly explained**, ask before approving.

If you want, I can also turn this into a **one-page printable checklist** or a **super-short version with checkboxes only**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, this is usable. Tight enough to scan, and it hits the stuff that matters — scope, parts, labor, pricing, authorization. The red flags section is the part I’d actually keep in front of me.

The only thing I’d add is a line for:
- [ ] Anything changed from the original quote is marked and initialed

That’s usually where they try to slide stuff in.

Otherwise, fine.
